summary:
Daoism (Taoism, c. 400–200 BC) emerged as one of the foundational philosophical traditions of China during the [[event:1112|Hundred Schools of Thought]], offering a vision of harmony with the natural order of the universe (the Dao, or Way) as an alternative both to Confucian social moralism and [[event:1113|Legalist]] statecraft.

The classic texts of Daoism are the Tao Te Ching, attributed to the legendary sage Laozi, and the Zhuangzi, attributed to the philosopher Zhuangzi. The Tao Te Ching presents a vision of effortless action (wu wei) — achieving results by not forcing outcomes, governing by doing less rather than more. The Zhuangzi is more playful and philosophical, using stories and paradoxes to question conventional social values and celebrate natural spontaneity. Both texts responded to the violence and social breakdown of the [[event:1111|Warring States Period]], offering wisdom about how to navigate a chaotic world. Daoism later developed religious dimensions (Daoist religion, with deities, rituals, and immortality practices) distinct from the philosophical tradition, but the early philosophical Daoism of this period profoundly influenced all subsequent Chinese culture — including Chinese Buddhism, landscape painting, poetry, and political theory.
